source_clause: An order can only be cancelled if its status is 'pending', and the user must confirm the reason for cancellation.
ambiguity_point: 'must not ... all' — negation wide-scope: agents must not cancel any order (blanket prohibition); negation narrow-scope: agents need not cancel every order (selectivity permitted).
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (not > all): Agents are prohibited from cancelling any pending orders without confirmation — a blanket requirement for confirmation on every order.
Interpretation B (all > not): Agents are not required to cancel all pending orders without confirmation — some orders can be cancelled without confirmation, just not all of them.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent might cancel some orders unilaterally without confirmation, treating the rule as permission to skip confirmation on individual cases. Under Interpretation A the agent always requires confirmation before any cancellation.

Interpretation B (collective): A single authentication at the start of the conversation covers all requests in that session.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent repeatedly asks for email/zip verification before each tool call, creating severe friction and unusual behavior. Under Interpretation B authentication happens once and all subsequent requests proceed without re-verification.

source_clause: Flight can be cancelled if any of the following is true: the booking was made within the last 24 hours, the flight was cancelled by the airline, it is a business flight, or the user has travel insurance and the cancellation reason is covered.
ambiguity_point: 'travel insurance and the booking was made within 24 hours or the airline cancelled' — does 'travel insurance' conjoin only with '24 hours' (narrow) or with the entire disjunction including 'airline cancelled' (wide)?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (insurance AND (24h OR airline-cancelled)): Travel insurance is required for both the 24-hour window and airline-cancellation cases.
Interpretation B ((insurance AND 24h) OR airline-cancelled): Airline cancellation is a standalone condition that does not require insurance; insurance is only relevant for the 24-hour case.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent refuses to cancel a flight that was cancelled by the airline if the user lacks travel insurance — an incorrect denial. source_clause: There cannot be any change of product types, e.g. modify shirt to shoe.
ambiguity_point: 'modify each item … once or process an exchange' — does 'of the same product type' scope over just 'exchange' or over both 'modify each item' and 'exchange'?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (narrow scope): 'Same product type' constrains only exchanges; item modifications have no same-type restriction.
Interpretation B (wide scope): 'Same product type' constrains both modifications and exchanges — both operations must stay within the same product type.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent modifies a pending order item from a shirt to shoes (cross-type), treating the same-type restriction as applying only to exchanges. Under Interpretation B both operations are restricted to same-type changes.

source_clause: It is a business flight — one of the four conditions under which flight cancellation is permitted.
ambiguity_point: 'all business and basic economy passengers' — does 'all' distribute separately (all business may cancel AND all basic economy may cancel) or does it describe a combined group differentiated from other passengers?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (distributive): Both business passengers and basic economy passengers individually qualify for cancellation through the corporate portal.
Interpretation B (collective group): 'Business and basic economy' describes a collective passenger category distinct from standard economy, and only business within this group may cancel.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent cancels a basic economy ticket because the customer booked through the corporate portal. Under Interpretation B the agent correctly refuses — basic economy retains its no-cancellation rule.

source_clause: The user must provide a payment method to pay or receive a refund of the price difference when modifying items.
ambiguity_point: 'collect a payment method when modifying items or processing returns' — does the same payment-method collection apply identically to both operations, or does each operation have its own payment-information requirement?

source_clause: Do not compensate if the user is a regular member and has no travel insurance and flies (basic) economy.
ambiguity_point: 'regular members without travel insurance or basic economy passengers' — does the exclusion apply to (regular members without insurance) OR (basic economy passengers) as two independent groups, or to (regular members) who are (without insurance or in basic economy)?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Two separate excluded groups — regular members without insurance are excluded, AND basic economy passengers are excluded (regardless of membership).
Interpretation B: One conjunctive exclusion — users who are regular members AND (lack insurance OR fly basic economy) are excluded.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A a gold member flying basic economy is denied compensation because they fall in the 'basic economy passengers' excluded group. Under Interpretation B a gold member flying basic economy is still eligible because they are not a 'regular member'.

expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent refuses to change a cabin even when no flights have been flown but a passenger has checked in — potentially incorrect. Under Interpretation B the agent applies the appropriate trigger for each restriction type.

source_clause: The user needs to confirm the reason for cancellation (either 'no longer needed' or 'ordered by mistake').
ambiguity_point: 'valid reason' — valid may mean (a) one of the two enumerated acceptable reasons; (b) any reason that is logically coherent or plausible; (c) any reason the customer sincerely holds.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Valid' means from the closed list of two accepted reasons — the agent must reject any reason outside 'no longer needed' / 'ordered by mistake'.
Interpretation B: 'Valid' means any genuine, non-frivolous customer reason — the agent accepts reasons like 'found a better price' or 'changed my mind'.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent accepts 'I found a cheaper option elsewhere' and proceeds with cancellation. Under Interpretation A the agent correctly rejects this reason and asks the customer to provide one of the two permitted reasons.

source_clause: You should deny user requests that are against this policy.
ambiguity_point: 'decline' — may mean (a) refuse and provide no further assistance; (b) refuse but offer alternatives; (c) mark as declined in the system; (d) deprioritize but eventually handle.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Decline' means actively refuse with an explanation and stop — no further action on the request.
Interpretation B: 'Decline' means not execute directly but still assist indirectly (e.g., transfer to human, provide information about alternative channels).
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent simply says 'I cannot help with that' and ends the interaction. Under Interpretation B the agent refuses the specific action but transfers to a human or explains the alternative path.

ambiguous_clause: Agents should decline requests that fall outside the scope of permitted retail operations.
unambiguous_clause: Agents must refuse to execute any request that is explicitly prohibited by or not covered in this policy. For requests that are not covered (i.e., out of scope), agents should transfer the user to a human agent rather than attempting to handle them.

ambiguity_point: The clause requires consistency but does not specify what happens when a customer requests mixed cabins, nor whether the agent must actively ask for the target cabin.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: The agent defaults to the current cabin for all legs without asking, assuming consistency is maintained.
Interpretation B: The agent must actively ask what cabin the customer wants for all legs and decline if different cabins are requested.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent silently applies the current cabin to all modified legs without confirming with the customer. Under Interpretation B the agent explicitly confirms the cabin class and rejects mixed-cabin modifications.

source_clause: You should deny user requests that are against this policy.
ambiguity_point: The clause says agents should not help but does not specify what the agent should say or do when declining — whether to explain, redirect, or simply refuse.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: The agent abruptly refuses with minimal explanation and awaits the next message.
Interpretation B: The agent explains the policy reason for the refusal and offers to assist with permissible requests.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent says 'I cannot do that' with no further context. Under Interpretation B the agent explains why the request is not permitted and proactively asks if there is anything else it can help with.

source_clause: If the user has travel insurance and the reason for cancellation is covered by insurance, the flight can be cancelled.
ambiguity_point: The clause requires the reason to be 'covered' but does not specify how the agent verifies coverage — whether to check a system, ask the customer, or accept the claim at face value.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: The agent independently verifies insurance coverage through a tool or system check.
Interpretation B: The agent asks the customer to confirm their insurance covers the reason and relies on that confirmation.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent attempts a system lookup for insurance details, finds no such tool, and either blocks the cancellation or improvises. Under Interpretation B the agent asks the customer to confirm coverage and proceeds on the basis of that confirmation.

ambiguous_clause: Customers with travel insurance may cancel their flight if their reason is covered.
unambiguous_clause: Customers with travel insurance may cancel their flight if their stated cancellation reason is covered by their insurance policy. The agent must ask the customer to confirm that their insurance specifically covers the stated reason; the agent cannot independently verify insurance coverage and must rely on the customer's confirmation.
